frameworks.name: executive attention / top-down control
frameworks.one_line: working memory capacity reflects executive attention biasing percept dominance

frameworks.name: bistable perception
frameworks.one_line: Necker cube alternations modulated by individual attentional control

frameworks.name: individual-differences psychometrics
frameworks.one_line: WMC variation predicting lower-level perceptual performance

core_question: Do individual differences in working memory capacity predict perception of a bistable Necker cube?
key_assumptions: top-down attention can bias bistable percept dominance
dominance durations reflect controllable perceptual states
manipulations: four viewing conditions (passive, hold-unspecific, hold-specific, reverse)
key_findings: WMC positively correlated with dominance duration in passive and hold-unspecific conditions
WMC negatively correlated with dominance duration in reverse condition
WMC negatively correlated with non-specified percept duration in hold-specific condition
interpretations: WMC differences relate to ability to exert top-down attentional control over bistable dominance
executive attention extends from cognitive into perceptual domains
